items.myth: Polynomial regression is a completely different algorithm from linear regression.
items.reality: It is linear regression applied to expanded features. The solver, loss, and regularization are the same; only the design matrix changes.
items.example: sklearn's `Pipeline([PolynomialFeatures(2), LinearRegression()])` uses the same `LinearRegression` class as a straight-line fit.

items.myth: A higher-degree polynomial is always a better model.
items.reality: Higher degree lowers training error but often raises validation error by fitting noise. The best degree balances bias and variance for your sample size.
items.example: On 15 noisy points along a gentle parabola, degree 2 generalizes; degree 12 wiggles through every point and predicts poorly at x = 0.5 between samples.

items.myth: Polynomial models are safe to extrapolate beyond the data range.
items.reality: Polynomials grow without bound (unless degree is capped and coefficients cancel). Predictions far outside observed x are usually meaningless.
items.example: A degree-3 fit on ages 25–55 may predict absurd income values at age 100 because x^3 dominates outside the training span.
